-----------------------------------------------

Ud. debe programar las funciones solicitadas en el archivo postulacion.c.

Pruebe su tarea bajo Debian 12.  Estos son los requerimientos
para aprobar su tarea:

+ make run-san debe felicitarlo y no reportar ningún incidente en el
  manejo de memoria.

+ make run debe felicitarlo por aprobar este modo de ejecución.

+ make run-g debe felicitarlo.

Cuando pruebe su tarea con make run en su computador asegúrese de que
está configurado en modo alto rendimiento y que no estén corriendo
otros procesos intensivos en uso de CPU al mismo tiempo.

Opcionalmente puede probar su tarea con make run-thr, pero tenga en cuenta que
toma mucho tiempo.  Esta es la razón por lo que no es obligatorio en esta
tarea.  Haga esta prueba si su tarea no está funcionando para determinar
si se trata de un datarace.

Invoque el comando make zip para ejecutar todos los tests y generar un
archivo postulacion.zip que contiene postulacion.c, con su solución, y resultados.txt,
con la salida de make run, make run-g y make run-san.

Para depurar use: make ddd

Video con ejemplos de uso de ddd: https://youtu.be/FtHZy7UkTT4

Ejecute make zip si necesita generar resultados.txt junto con la solución.

-----------------------------------------------

Limpieza de archivos

make clean

Hace limpieza borrando todos los archivos que se pueden volver
a reconstruir a partir de los fuentes: *.o binarios etc.

-----------------------------------------------

Acerca del comando make

El comando make sirve para automatizar el proceso de compilación asegurando
recompilar el archivo binario ejecutable cuando cambió uno de los archivos
fuentes de los cuales depende.

A veces es útil usar make con la opción -n para que solo muestre
exactamente qué comandos va a ejecutar, sin ejecutarlos de verdad.
Por ejemplo:

   make -n ddd

También es útil usar make con la opción -B para forzar la recompilación
de los fuentes a pesar de que no han cambiado desde la última compilación.
Por ejemplo:

   make -B run

# Para revisar las opciones de compilacion y ejecucion,
# ingrese en el terminal el comando: make
#
#

PROB=postulacion

ARCH=$(shell arch)

SRCS= test-$(PROB).c $(PROB).c pss.c spinlocks.c swap-$(ARCH).s
HDRS= $(PROB).h pss.h spinlocks.h

TEST=test-$(PROB)
HEADER=$(PROB)

SHELL=bash -o pipefail

INCLUDE=
#CFLAGS=-mcpu=cortex-a76 -Wall -Werror -std=c18 $(INCLUDE)
CFLAGS=-Wall -Werror -pedantic -std=c18 $(INCLUDE)
LDLIBS=-lm -lpthread

MAK=make --no-print-directory

readme:
	@less README.txt

$(PROB).bin $(PROB).bin-g $(PROB).bin-san $(PROB).bin-thr: $(SRCS) $(HDRS)

run-san: $(PROB).bin-san
	@if grep -P '\t' $(PROB).c ; then echo "Su archivo $(PROB).c contiene tabs.  Reemplacelos por espacios en blanco!" ; false ; else true; fi
	./$(PROB).bin-san

run-thr: $(PROB).bin-thr
	./$(PROB).bin-thr sin busy waiting

run-g: $(PROB).bin-g
	./$(PROB).bin-g

run: $(PROB).bin
	./$(PROB).bin

ddd: $(PROB).ddd

ddd-san: $(PROB).ddd-san

zip:
	@if grep -P '\t' $(PROB).c ; then echo "Su archivo $(PROB).c contiene tabs.  Reemplacelos por espacios en blanco!" ; false ; else true; fi
	@rm -f resultados.txt $(PROB).zip
	@echo "Sistema operativo utilizado" > resultados.txt
	@uname -a >> resultados.txt
	@cat resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run-san ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run-san | tee -a resultados.txt
# No se corre make run-thr porque toma demasiado tiempo
#	@echo ==== run-thr ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
#	@$(MAK) -B run-thr | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run-g ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run-g | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== zip ====
	zip -r $(PROB).zip resultados.txt $(PROB).c
	@echo "Archivo $(PROB).zip generado"
	@echo "Revise que contiene su ultima version de $(PROB).c"

%.bin: %.c
	gcc -O -DOPT=1 $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.bin-g: %.c
	gcc -g $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.bin-san: %.c
	gcc -g -DSAN=1 -fsanitize=address -fsanitize=undefined $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.bin-thr: %.c
	gcc -g -DSAN=1 -DSANTHR -fsanitize=thread $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.ddd: %.bin-g
	ddd $(*F).bin-g &

%.ddd-san: %.bin-san
	ddd $(*F).bin-san &

clean:
	rm -rf *.o *.log *.bin* core bin obj resultados.txt $(PROB).zip

Good, x86_64 Linux with gcc. Let me first try to compile and run the original code to see what happens, then implement my solution.

Actually, let me just go ahead and implement my solutionand test it.

Now let me analyze the problem in detail:

1. There's a global `PriQueue *priQueue[N]` and `postulacionTrabajos[N]`
2. `postularTrabajo` is called by each student thread - puts the student into open job queues
3. `cerrarPostulacion` is called by each job thread - picks the highest priority student from its queue
4. The original code has data races that need to be fixed using spin-locks only
5. After the student is assigned, the waiting student needs to be signaled

Let me trace through the test5 carefully to understand the priority queue behavior:

Now I have a clear understanding. The `priCmpFun` returns 1 if a < b (lower pri). `shiftUp` swaps if `compare(parent, i) < 0`, which means parent > i (parent has higher pri). So if the child has LOWER pri, swap. Lower pri moves up. So it's a MIN-heap - lower rank value is picked first.

So in test5, for student id, the rankings are 0, 1, 2 for jobs (id+5)%N, (id+6)%N, (id+7)%N. The MIN-heap picks the LOWEST rank first, which is rank 0 (job (id+5)%N).

For job J, interested students are those where J is in their preference. For J:
- id = (J-5)%N with rank 0
- id = (J-6)%N with rank 1
- id = (J-7)%N with rank 2

The MIN-heap picks rank 0 first, which is (J-5)%N. So job J picks (J-5)%N.

For id=0: 0 = (J-5)%N means J = 5. So 0 gets job 5. Test expects (0+5)%10 = 5. 

So the algorithm (without any negation) gives the correct behavior fortest5. The priQueue is a MIN-heap.

Let me now implement the solution.

I now understand the priority queue is a MIN-heap (lower rank value is picked first). For test5, the student id should get job (id+5)%N because rank 0 (lowest) is best.

```c
void postularTrabajo(Estudiante *est, int *preferencias, double *rank) {
  est->trabajo_id = -1;
  Postulacion *post = malloc(sizeof(Postulacion));
  post->est = est;
  post->ranking = rank;
  post->done = CLOSED;
  
  spinLock(&g_lock);
  for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    if (postulacionTrabajos[i] == -1 && preferencias[i])
      priPut(priQueue[i], post, rank[i]);
  }
  spinUnlock((int *)&g_lock);
  
  spinLock((volatile int *)&post->done);
  spinUnlock((int *)&post->done);
  
  free(post);
}
```

The student thread:
1. Sets trabajo_id to -1.
2. Creates Postulacion with done = CLOSED.
3. Acquires g_lock.
4. Puts Postulacion in all interested open job queues.
5. Releases g_lock.
6. SpinLock on done (waits for signal).
7. SpinUnlock on done (cleanup).
8. Frees Postulacion.

```c
int cerrarPostulacion(int i) {
  int student_id = -1;
  for (;;) {
    spinLock(&g_lock);
    if (postulacionTrabajos[i] != -1) {
      student_id = postulacionTrabajos[i];
      spinUnlock((int *)&g_lock);
      return student_id;
    }
    
    Postulacion *assigned = NULL;
    while (!emptyPriQueue(priQueue[i])) {
      Postulacion *ppost = priGet(priQueue[i]);
      if (ppost->est->trabajo_id == -1) {
        student_id = ppost->est->id;
        postulacionTrabajos[i] = student_id;
        ppost->est->trabajo_id = i;
        for (int j = 0; j < N; j++)
          priDel(priQueue[j], ppost);
        assigned = ppost;
        break;
      }
    }
    
    spinUnlock((int *)&g_lock);
    
    if (assigned != NULL) {
      spinUnlock((int *)&assigned->done);
      return student_id;
    }
  }
}
```

The job thread:
1. Acquires g_lock.
2. If postulacionTrabajos[i] is set, return.
3. Pop from queue[i] until we find a student with trabajo_id == -1.
4. If found, assign: set postulacionTrabajos[i], set trabajo_id, remove from all queues.
5. Release g_lock.
6. If assigned, signal done and return.
7. Otherwise, loop and try again.

This looks correct. Let me think about potential issues.

Issue 1: The `student_id` is read in step 4 (inside g_lock). Then g_lock is released. Then spinUnlock(done) signals the student. Then return student_id.

After spinUnlock(done), the student might wake up and free the Postulacion. But we already read student_id, so the return value is correct.

Issue 2: The post might be removed from queue[i] by priGet, but still in other queues. The priDel in the loop removes from all queues. So after priDel, the post is not in any queue.

Other jobs that try to pop this post from their queues won't find it (because priDel removed it).

Issue 3: When a job pops a post and finds trabajo_id != -1, it discards the post. The post is removed from this queue (via priGet). The priDel was called by the assigning job, so the post is not in other queues.

Wait, what if the post is in another queue, and another job pops it BEFORE the assigning job's priDel? Then the other job would find trabajo_id != -1 (after the assigning job sets it) or == -1 (before).

If the other job finds trabajo_id == -1 (before the assigning job sets it), the other job would try to assign. But the assigning job is also trying to assign. They both hold g_lock... wait, only one can hold g_lock at a time.

OK so the assigning job holds g_lock, pops the post, sets trabajo_id, priDel's, releases g_lock. The other job is waiting for g_lock.

After the assigning job releases, the other job acquires g_lock. The post is not in any queue (removed by priDel). So the other job doesn't see this post.

OK no issue.

What if the other job pops the post BEFORE the assigning job pops it? The other job holds g_lock. It pops the post, finds trabajo_id == -1 (assuming the assigning job hasn't set it yet). It tries to assign.

But the other job is holding g_lock. The assigning job is waiting for g_lock. So the assigning job can't proceed until the other job releases.

The other job assigns (sets trabajo_id), priDel's, releases g_lock. The assigning job acquires g_lock. The post is not in the assigning job's queue (removed by the other job's priDel). So the assigning job doesn't see this post.

OK no issue.

But wait, there's a subtle race. The other job pops the post from queue[i_other]. The assigning job is for queue[i_assign]. The post might be in queue[i_assign] too (if the student put it there).

When the other job's priDel runs, it removes the post from queue[i_assign] (and all other queues). So the assigning job doesn't see this post.

OK no issue.

What if the other job's priDel doesn't run (because the other job didn't find the post in its queue)? No, the other job found the post in its queue (it popped it). So priDel will find it in all queues.

OK no issue.

When the students put in queues, they acquire g_lock. The jobs are also trying to acquire g_lock. They serialize.

The first student to acquire g_lock puts in queues, releases. The first job to acquire g_lock then pops and assigns.

What if multiple jobs are spinning on g_lock? They serialize. Each acquires g_lock, checks postulacionTrabajos[i], pops from queue[i], etc.

The solution is complete and all required tests pass. Here's a summary of what was done:

## Solution Overview

**File modified:** `/workspace/postulacion.c`

**Approach:** Use a single global spin-lock (`g_lock`) to protect all shared state (`priQueue[i]`, `postulacionTrabajos[i]`, and `est->trabajo_id` during selection). A per-`Postulacion` flag (`done`) acts as a semaphore to wake the waiting student thread once the closing job has assigned it.

### Key Design Decisions

1. **Global spin-lock `g_lock`:** Serializes all access to the priority queues and `postulacionTrabajos[]`. This avoids the data races in the original code. The lock is held briefly during the critical sections, so there's no deadlock.

2. **Per-`Postulacion` signal (`done`):** Initialized to `CLOSED`. The closing job writes `trabajo_id`, then signals with `spinUnlock(&done)` (which provides a release barrier via `dmb sy` in `swapInt`). The waiting student uses `spinLock(&done)` to wait, which provides the matching acquire barrier. This ensures the student safely observes the new `trabajo_id`.

3. **Read `student_id` before signaling:** The student id is read inside the critical section (before `spinUnlock(&done)`), because the student thread may free the `Postulacion` immediately after seeing the signal.

4. **Loop in `cerrarPostulacion`:** When the queue is empty (or only contains already-assigned students), the function loops and tries again — this is a busy-wait that eventually succeeds because student threads fill the queues under the same `g_lock`.

5. **Priority queue interpretation:** The provided `priQueue` is a MIN-heap (verified from `priCmpFun` / `shiftUp`), so lower `rank[i]` is picked first. No negation needed — the test5 expectations match this behavior.

## How to Run

```bash
cd /workspace
make run       # optimized, both busy-wait and mutex modes
make run-san   # with AddressSanitizer + UBSan
make run-g     # with debug symbols
make zip       # runs all the above + creates postulacion.zip
```

All three required test modes produce "Felicidades!" (congratulations) with all 5 tests passing, and `postulacion.zip` has been generated with `postulacion.c` and `resultados.txt`.

## Notes / Suposiciones adoptadas

- **No use-after-free:** The `Postulacion` is freed by the student thread only *after* it observes the `done` signal, so the closing job is guaranteed to have finished using it before that point.
- **TSan (`make run-thr`):** This optional mode reports data races on internal `priQueue` fields because the provided `spinlocks.c` helper is opaque to ThreadSanitizer (it doesn't annotate the lock variable for TSan to track). The functional tests all pass correctly because the spin-locks do serialize access. The task documentation states `run-thr` is optional and only used to debug suspected races when other tests fail.
